name: TopBuzz
description: ByteDance's overseas news and video app, the English-language Toutiao. An early pre-TikTok bet, it shut in 2020 as users drained away.
killedReason: Discontinued by ByteDance for chronic underperformance in the Western/overseas news market. After peaking near 36-40M MAU and ~7M combined App Store + Google Play downloads in 2018, downloads collapsed to ~1.2M in H1 2019 and to only ~20,000 globally in April 2020, with the app falling out of the top of the US App Store news category against rivals like News Break. ByteDance had unsuccessfully tried to sell TopBuzz to outside buyers in 2019, then began a gradual wind-down (halting new app versions, tapering article updates) before confirming the shutdown on Friday, June 5, 2020, saying it was "proud of the work that we accomplished with TopBuzz, but have determined that other areas of the business should be our priority going forward." The move reflected ByteDance's pivot to prioritize TikTok (and, in China, Toutiao). It was part of a broader 2020 culling of underperforming overseas apps (e.g., the video app Vigo was shut down days later on June 15, 2020). SEPARATELY and AFTER the shutdown: a July 26, 2022 BuzzFeed News investigation reported allegations from four former employees that ByteDance had used TopBuzz to insert "soft" pro-China content (panda videos, China-travel promotion) and to suppress content critical of the Chinese government, Hong Kong protests and Xi Jinping; ByteDance called the allegations "false and ridiculous." These allegations post-date and were NOT the stated cause of the shutdown.
region: Global / overseas, primarily US and UK (English-language); localized expansions to Japan (~Sept 2016) and Brazil (2016, incl. TopBuzz Video). Not offered in mainland China, where the equivalent product is Toutiao. At the June 2020 announcement the Japanese version was still listed on Google Play (per Reuters/Caixin), so regional removal lagged by days.
publisher: App Store: "Bytedance Incorporation" / "Bytedance Inc." (App Store id 1049682098) | Google Play: "TopBuzz Inc." | TOPBUZZ trademark owner: Beijing Bytedance Technology Co., Ltd. (北京字节跳动科技有限公司)

notes: Independently re-verified from scratch; the draft was already highly accurate. Genuine ByteDance product (overseas Toutiao), confirmed dead, shutdown announced/pulled June 5, 2020. KEY CORRECTION: the alias "顶尖新闻" (Dǐngjiān Xīnwén) is NOT an official name — every Chinese-language source (VOA Chinese, 36Kr, Baidu Baike, Zhihu) calls it "今日头条海外版" / "海外版今日头条" (Toutiao overseas version). Demoted "顶尖新闻" to an explicitly-flagged informal/back-translated alias and elevated "今日头条海外版" as the standard descriptor. The app was an English-language overseas product with no official mainland-China Chinese-market name (that role was Toutiao's). OTHER NOTES: (1) Launch August 2015 confirmed by multiple Chinese sources + Baidu Baike; App Store id 1049682098 (registered ~Sep 2015) verified live via SearchMan/AppAdvice with seller "Bytedance Incorporation." (2) June 5, 2020 was a Friday — SCMP/Caixin/Reuters all reference the "Friday" announcement, consistent. Wind-down was gradual (2019 sale attempts, halted versions, tapering content), so no single clean "last day of service"; the Japanese version was still on Google Play at announcement. (3) MAU varies by source: own site claimed 36M; BuzzFeed cited 40M (2018, primarily US/UK). (4) Publisher split confirmed: App Store "Bytedance Incorporation"/"Bytedance Inc."; Google Play "TopBuzz Inc." (5) Pro-China content allegations are from BuzzFeed News (publication date July 26, 2022; widely re-reported July 27), disputed by ByteDance, post-date the shutdown — context only, not the kill reason. (6) TopBuzz briefly topped the NA App Store news category in March 2017; Vigo was shut down days later (June 15, 2020) as part of the same overseas-app culling.

region: India, Brazil, Middle East, Indonesia, Bangladesh (overseas markets only — never operated in China)
description: ByteDance's overseas app for 15-second clips, built from its Hypstar and Flipagram buys. ByteDance closed it in 2020 and moved users to TikTok.
killedReason: Strategic consolidation into TikTok. On June 14, 2020 ByteDance announced it would shut Vigo Video and Vigo Lite to focus resources on other businesses, asking users to migrate content to flagship TikTok before the apps closed by October 31, 2020. The apps had massively underperformed TikTok and were closed market by market — Brazil and the Middle East first (described as 'already discontinued' by the June 2020 announcement, i.e. early 2020), then India by October 31, 2020. The wind-down coincided with India's June 29, 2020 ban of 59 Chinese apps, which included both Vigo Video and TikTok, prompting removal from Indian app stores before the formal global shutdown. Separately, BuzzFeed News later reported (March 2022, citing four former employees) that ByteDance had used fake accounts seeded with content scraped from Instagram, Snapchat and its own Chinese apps to populate Flipagram/Vigo in 2017 — context for the product's troubled history, though not the stated reason for closure.
notes: VERIFIED ACCURATE. Confidence HIGH on: ByteDance ownership (publisher BYTEMOD PTE. LTD. confirmed via Sensor Tower; Hypstar acquired July 2017, Flipagram acquired January 2017), dead status, India shutdown date (Oct 31, 2020, announced June 14, 2020), iOS App Store ID 1249798762, Android package com.ss.android.ugc.boom, India 59-app-ban inclusion (June 29, 2020 — Vigo Video explicitly named on the list), Brazil/Middle East closing first, user counts (~4M Vigo Video, ~1.5M Vigo Lite vs ~200M TikTok), TikTok-consolidation rationale, and the BuzzFeed/Flipagram scraping story (TechCrunch/BuzzFeed, surfaced March 2022). MEDIUM on launch date: 2017-07 reflects the ByteDance-era launch as Hypstar (acquired July 2017); the underlying Hypstar/Flipagram products predate this (Flipagram dates to 2013) and no source gives an exact day — 2017-07 is best-supported. MEDIUM on Brazil/Middle East shutdown timing: every source says these were closed before the June 14, 2020 India announcement ('already discontinued'); 'early 2020' is consistent but no precise date found. The 2017–2018 'Vigo Video' rebrand (Hypstar + acquired Flipagram unified) is supported by secondary sources but ByteDance issued no formal public rebrand announcement, so the exact rebrand date is fuzzy. IMPORTANT distinction confirmed: the China-side counterpart Huoshan (火山小视频) was a SEPARATE domestic app that did NOT shut down — it was renamed Douyin Huoshan (抖音火山版) in January 2020 and merged into Douyin's ecosystem. Do not conflate domestic Huoshan (alive/merged) with overseas Vigo (dead). The 火山小视频 entry in aliases is retained only as a cross-reference and is flagged as a distinct app.

name: Flipagram
category: Short-form video / photo-video slideshow social app
region: US / Global (originally HQ Los Angeles, USA)
killedReason: Acquired by ByteDance's Toutiao (今日头条) in early 2017 (announced Feb 1, 2017, undisclosed price). After ByteDance also bought rival Musical.ly in late 2017 and merged it into TikTok, Flipagram became redundant. ByteDance laid off the Los Angeles-based Flipagram team, removed the original Flipagram iOS app from the App Store on July 17, 2018, and folded/rebranded the product into Vigo Video (combined with its Hypstar/Hipstar app; the China-market sibling was Huoshan 火山小视频). Vigo Video itself was later wound down: closure announced June 15, 2020, with the India shutdown set for Oct 31, 2020 (Brazil and Middle East had already ceased earlier), as ByteDance consolidated resources behind TikTok; users were directed to export content to TikTok. Flipagram/Vigo was also at the center of a BuzzFeed News investigation (published April 2022) alleging ByteDance used fake accounts and content scraped from Instagram/Snapchat starting shortly after the Jan/Feb 2017 acquisition to artificially grow the app.

(high), acquisition announced Feb 1 2017 (high), original iOS app removed July 17 2018 (used as dateKilled; medium-high — sourced to productmint/buildd/tamaggo secondary sources, not a primary Apple record), Vigo closure announced June 15 2020 / India end Oct 31 2020 (high, TechNode + multiple Indian press). MEDIUM/LOW confidence on the Flipagram Labs Inc. revival being connected to original founders — a flipagram.app site and trademark filing exist, but no primary source confirms an original founder re-acquired the IP from ByteDance; treat as plausible but unverified. Beware unrelated namesakes (Microsoft Flipgrid, "Flipview Flipagram", "Picflix - Flipagram Slideshow") which are NOT this product.

description: The 15-second lip-sync app that defined teen short video before TikTok. ByteDance bought it in 2017 and merged its ~100M users into TikTok in 2018.
killedReason: Strategic consolidation by parent company ByteDance. After acquiring Musical.ly in November 2017, ByteDance merged it into its sister app TikTok (international Douyin) to combine Musical.ly's strong Western/US user base with TikTok's larger Asian audience and recommendation algorithm, and to run a single global platform instead of two overlapping apps. The Musical.ly brand was retired and all users/content migrated to TikTok. In the official TikTok newsroom announcement, co-founder Alex Zhu framed it as a natural fit given the shared mission of both apps ("excited to enter into a new chapter"); a frequently cited quote about supporting one platform to expand local market presence is attributed in press coverage to TikTok marketing head Stefan Heinrich, not to the official newsroom release.
notes: Independently re-verified from scratch; all major facts confirmed by multiple independent sources (Wikipedia, official TikTok newsroom, Bloomberg, TechCrunch, NPR, Variety, Hollywood Reporter, Billboard, Pandaily). Founders (Alex Zhu, Luyu Yang), Shanghai HQ, user milestones (90M Jun 2016, 200M May 2017, ~100M MAU at merger), and the $800M–$1B acquisition price are well-corroborated; the exact dollar figure was never officially disclosed (range, not a point value). ByteDance acquisition: Nov 10, 2017 (announced Nov 9 US time). Merger/shutdown: official TikTok newsroom announcement dated Aug 1, 2018; migration and press coverage Aug 2, 2018 — dateKilled given as 2018-08-02 (consummation/migration). Status correctly 'merged' not 'dead': accounts/content auto-migrated, brand retired, and Musical.ly Inc. was renamed TikTok Inc. (in-place rebrand). Dropped invented alias '穆西卡尔'; dateLaunched downgraded to 2014-08 (only 'August 2014' firmly sourced); Stefan Heinrich quote is press coverage, not the official release. App Store id 835599320 plausible but not live-verified.
verifyNotes: VERDICT: Genuine ByteDance product — KEEP. isByteDance=true with high confidence (ByteDance acquired Musical.ly Inc. Nov 10, 2017 and merged it into TikTok Aug 1–2, 2018; Musical.ly Inc. renamed TikTok Inc.). Status 'merged' is correct — not deleted; users/content auto-migrated into TikTok, brand retired. The app does NOT still operate under its own name. Launch: August 2014 (prototype April 2014); draft's '2014-08-01' day-precision downgraded to '2014-08' as the exact day isn't reliably sourced. Kill/merger date 2018-08-02 confirmed accurate (newsroom Aug 1, migration Aug 2). description: Musical.ly's live-streaming app for broadcasting video and earning gifts. ByteDance closed it in 2018 and folded the streaming into Musical.ly.
killedReason: Contractually required by ByteDance's November 2017 Musical.ly acquisition: a clause obligated Live.ly to shut down within six months and redirect users to LiveMe for at least 30 days, because ByteDance held a ~$50M stake in rival live-streamer LiveMe and would not run two competing properties. The standalone app was announced dead June 13, 2018 and pulled from app stores ~June 15, 2018; its live-streaming was absorbed into Musical.ly, which merged into TikTok on August 2, 2018.
notes: Qualifies for a "Killed by ByteDance" graveyard: Live.ly was not built in-house by ByteDance, but it was ByteDance-owned at end of life and its death was directly caused by ByteDance (the Nov 2017 Musical.ly acquisition contractually required Live.ly to shut down within 6 months and redirect users to LiveMe, in which ByteDance held a ~$50M stake). dateKilled is 2018-06, NOT 2018-08-02: the August 2, 2018 date is the separate Musical.ly→TikTok merger/rebrand; the Live.ly standalone app was announced dead June 13, 2018 and pulled ~June 15, 2018 (Engadget, Digital Music News, TechCrunch). Exact day it went fully offline is documented only as ~June 13–15, 2018. App Store ID 1085024470 corroborated by Sensor Tower and Apptopia (original developer Musical.ly Inc.). Install metrics reconcile: ~26M total installs, ~70% US, ~87% iOS (different metrics, all consistent); ~40M active users at peak; hit #1 on US App Store. Acquisition value reported ~$800M–$1B across sources.

name: Resso
description: ByteDance's social music app for emerging markets, built around scrolling lyrics. Folded into TikTok Music abroad; India pulled it in 2024 after a data probe.
killedReason: Wound down and partly rebranded as TikTok Music, then forced out of its last market by Indian regulators. Brazil and Indonesia migrated to ByteDance's new TikTok Music service (launched there July 6, 2023), with Resso discontinued in both countries on September 5, 2023. In India, the Ministry of Electronics and IT (MeitY) probed ByteDance's handling of Indian user data and use of proprietary algorithms; the government ordered Apple and Google to delist Resso in December 2023. Rather than respond, ByteDance exited, shutting Resso in India effective January 31, 2024 (citing "local market conditions") with refunds for remaining subscriptions, and never launched TikTok Music there. The TikTok Music successor was itself shut down globally on November 28, 2024, ending ByteDance's standalone music-streaming line entirely.

name: TikTok Music
description: ByteDance's paid music service and Resso's successor, tied to TikTok. ByteDance closed it after 16 months to steer listeners to Spotify and Apple Music.
killedReason: ByteDance chose to stop running its own standalone streaming service and instead drive music engagement through partner platforms via TikTok's "Add to Music App" feature, which lets users save tracks discovered on TikTok to Spotify, Apple Music, and Amazon Music. Ole Obermann, TikTok's Global Head of Music Business Development, said the company was "closing TikTok Music at the end of November in order to focus on our goal of furthering TikTok's role in driving even greater music listening and value on music streaming services, for the benefit of artists, songwriters, and the industry." Contributing factors widely cited in the press: the standalone service failed to gain traction against entrenched incumbents (Spotify/Apple Music) and never expanded to large markets like the US/UK, and ByteDance faced costly music-licensing tensions (Universal Music Group's recordings license lapsed February 1, 2024 and its catalog was pulled from TikTok before a new deal was reached on May 2, 2024).

name: Helo
publisher: Helo Holdings Limited

appStoreId: 1437494436 (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/helo-share-your-life/id1437494436)
description: ByteDance's social app for India, sharing memes and Bollywood clips in 14-plus languages. India banned it in 2020; ByteDance killed it in 2021 when it quit India.
killedReason: Banned by the Indian government. On 29 June 2020 India blocked Helo as one of 59 Chinese-origin apps (alongside TikTok, UC Browser, SHAREit, Likee, CamScanner, etc.) under Section 69A of the IT Act, citing data-security and national-sovereignty concerns following the deadly 15 June 2020 Galwan Valley India-China border clash. The interim ban was made permanent in late January 2021 (MeitY decision reported 25-26 Jan 2021, after the government found company responses to its queries unsatisfactory). With India being Helo's sole market, ByteDance announced on 27 January 2021 that it was winding down its India business and cutting its workforce (2,000+ employees affected), effectively killing the app.

killedReason: Underperformance and a strategic retreat from book publishing. It never sold titles through TikTok Shop and barely promoted books organically on the platform (reportedly one organic video, leaning on paid ads), exposing ByteDance's inexperience in traditional publishing. Editorial Director Allison Moore left in April 2025; agents and authors were told around May 2025 that rights would be returned, and the closure was reported in June 2025. ByteDance declined to comment, and the imprint's website and social accounts were deleted afterward.

name: TikTok Lite Rewards
aliases: TikTok Lite Task and Reward programme
description: A watch-to-earn feature in TikTok Lite paying users points to watch and invite friends. EU regulators flagged addiction risks; ByteDance pulled it in 2024.
killedReason: Killed by EU regulation. The European Commission scrutinized the programme under the Digital Services Act (DSA), concerned it was launched without the legally required prior risk assessment and that its watch-to-earn mechanics could stimulate addictive behaviour and harm users' (especially minors') mental and physical health. After a 17 April 2024 request for information and the opening of formal proceedings on 22 April 2024 (Case DSA.100121), TikTok voluntarily suspended the Rewards programme on 24 April 2024. On 5 August 2024 the Commission made TikTok's commitment to permanently withdraw the TikTok Lite Rewards programme from the entire EU legally binding — the first case the Commission closed under the DSA and the first time it accepted binding commitments from a designated very large online platform.
region: EU (launched in France and Spain; permanently withdrawn across the entire European Union)

ByteDance Ltd. (ultimate parent)

notes: Confidence high on all key facts. EXACT DATES: Permanent withdrawal made legally binding 5 August 2024 (primary: EC press release IP/24/4161 and EC digital-strategy press release; Commission Decision C/2024/5942 of 5 Aug 2024). Voluntary suspension 24 April 2024 (confirmed by EC, TechCrunch, CNN, Bloomberg); formal proceedings opened 22 April 2024 (Case DSA.100121, per EC); EC request-for-information sent 17 April 2024. LAUNCH DATE: Sources agree on April 2024 for the Rewards programme in France and Spain (TechCrunch explicitly: ByteDance "launched this version of TikTok Lite in France and Spain earlier this month" — i.e. April); a few outlets loosely say the TikTok Lite app itself rolled out in "March 2024," but the Rewards feature that was killed is consistently dated to April 2024 — hence dateLaunched given as 2024-04 (exact day not pinned by sources). SCOPING NOTE: This is a FEATURE/service that was killed, NOT a standalone app — the TikTok Lite app (App Store id 6447160980; Google Play com.tiktok.lite.go / com.zhiliaoapp.musically.go) remains alive outside the EU; only the watch-to-earn Rewards programme was permanently withdrawn from the EU. Type recorded as "service." Whether to include in a "graveyard" is a judgement call since the parent app lives on, but the Rewards programme is genuinely, permanently dead in the EU and is a notable EU-regulatory kill.

killedReason: NOT dead/discontinued — TikTok remains alive and operating globally; it was never shut down by ByteDance. "Banned" applies only to specific jurisdictions, never globally. Key restrictions: (1) INDIA — banned June 29, 2020 alongside 58 other Chinese apps after the Galwan Valley border clash; made permanent in January 2021 after the government rejected the companies' privacy/security responses (still banned). (2) US — the Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act (PAFACA) required ByteDance divestiture or ban; the Supreme Court upheld PAFACA on January 17, 2025 (TikTok, Inc. v. Garland); the app went dark for US users ~Jan 18–19, 2025 (~12 hrs) and was pulled from app stores, then restored after Trump pledged an executive order delaying enforcement. A "qualified divestiture" was reached: binding agreements signed Dec 18, 2025; TikTok USDS Joint Venture LLC (Oracle, Silver Lake, MGX each ~15%) closed January 22, 2026, giving US/international investors ~80.1% and ByteDance below 20%. (3) Other bans: Afghanistan (Taliban, ~April 2022, active); Senegal (Aug 2023, political unrest); Somalia (Aug 2023, indecent content/misinformation); Nepal (banned Nov 13, 2023, lifted Aug 22, 2024); Albania (one-year ban after a Nov 2024 school stabbing, took effect ~March 2025, expired/lifted Feb 5, 2026; ruled unconstitutional by Albania's Constitutional Court March 11, 2026).
notes: GRAVEYARD-FIT WARNING: TikTok is ByteDance's flagship and is ALIVE — not dead/discontinued/merged/globally-banned. For a "Killed by ByteDance" site this record would normally be DROPPED (status=alive). It belongs only if the site intends to document region-specific bans (India permanent, Afghanistan) or the musical.ly merger as a sub-graveyard entry. Other caveats: (1) The alias "抖音国际版 / Douyin International" is informal/marketing only — TikTok and Douyin are technically SEPARATE apps. (2) US JV: binding agreements signed Dec 18, 2025; deal CLOSED Jan 22, 2026 (NOT 2025). (3) Albania ban was lifted/expired Feb 5, 2026; Constitutional Court ruled it unconstitutional Mar 11, 2026. (4) International launch confirmed as September 2017 (exact day not pinned by primary sources); Douyin launched September 2016 in China. (5) musical.ly acquired Nov 9, 2017, merged into TikTok Aug 2, 2018 — confirmed. (6) Could not re-confirm Apple developer entity via App Store fetch (returned a China-region page); developer "TikTok Ltd." (id1322881000) and Google Play "TikTok Pte. Ltd." retained as plausible but flagged for double-check.

killedReason: Removed from the US App Store and Google Play on January 19, 2025 under the Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act (PAFACA), which barred distribution of ByteDance-owned apps in the US after ByteDance failed to divest. Apple's support notice listed Lemon8 among 11 ByteDance apps removed (alongside TikTok, TikTok Studio, TikTok Shop Seller Center, CapCut, Hypic, Gauth, Marvel Snap, and three Lark apps). Restored Feb 13, 2025 after President Trump's executive order delaying enforcement and AG Pam Bondi's written non-enforcement assurance to Apple and Google. The Jan 23, 2026 TikTok USDS Joint Venture announcement said its safeguards "will also cover CapCut, and Lemon8" in the US (ByteDance retains 19.9% of the JV). Status became contested again in early March 2026: TechTimes, iDropNews and 9to5Mac (sourcing Wired) reported Apple geoblocking Lemon8 alongside CapCut/Lark/Hypic as still-ByteDance-owned, while heise.de reported the opposite, that TikTok, CapCut and Lemon8 remain available and only Chinese-market apps (Douyin, Doubao, Fanqie Novel) are blocked. The app was never globally shut down and remains live and downloadable outside the US.

Ltd. is a ByteDance entity; App Store listing branded "powered by TikTok"; on Apple's official PAFACA ByteDance-app removal list). Core US ban timeline (removed Jan 19 2025, restored Feb 13 2025) is HIGH confidence. Two date clarifications: launched in Japan in 2020 as "Sharee" (May per Wikipedia/SCMP, March per some CN sources), renamed Lemon8 in September 2021 — dateLaunched set to 2020 for the Sharee origin. Overall confidence is MEDIUM because the current (June 2026) live US status is genuinely contradictory across sources: heise.de says Lemon8/TikTok/CapCut are available and only Chinese-market apps are blocked, while TechTimes/iDropNews/9to5Mac (citing Wired) list Lemon8 among geoblocked apps, and metricool (Jan 30 2026) says it is stable under the JV. status=banned and dateKilled=2025-01-19 retained because the PAFACA US removal is the defining graveyard event, scoped to the US market; the app is unambiguously alive globally. 12.5M Dec-2024 MAU is a single Sensor Tower estimate. Recommend a fresh status check before publishing — fast-moving regulatory situation.

Reporting on the March 2026 geoblocking (9to5Mac) specifically named CapCut, Lemon8, Lark and Hypic; Gauth was not individually named, and its US App Store listing continued to show updates into mid-2026 (e.g., a June 3, 2026 update per app-analytics trackers), so Gauth's precise US download status in 2026 is contested/fluid. name: Marvel Snap
description: A hit free-to-play card game by US studio Second Dinner, published worldwide by ByteDance's Nuverse. It lives on, but dropped ByteDance for Skystone Games in 2025.
killedReason: What died was ByteDance's publishing role, not the game. ByteDance announced its exit from mainstream gaming and the wind-down of Nuverse on Nov 27, 2023, but Marvel Snap kept running under the Nuverse label. Then on Jan 19, 2025, the game's US servers went dark and the app was pulled from the US App Store and Google Play under the Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act (PAFACA) — the same TikTok-divestiture law — because publisher Nuverse was a ByteDance subsidiary. Servers were restored ~Jan 21 and the app returned to US stores ~Jan 27-29. On Jan 28, 2025, Second Dinner announced it had ended the ByteDance/Nuverse arrangement, brought most operations in-house, and signed US-based Skystone Games as its new publisher — ending ByteDance's involvement.
region: Global. The US App Store / Google Play removal and server outage applied only to the United States (~Jan 19-29, 2025) and was fully reversed.
publisher: Nuverse (ByteDance's gaming subsidiary) — App Store/Google Play publisher entity from the Oct 2022 launch until Jan 2025. Store/registered entity has appeared as "Nuverse" and "Nverse Game" (CBInsights lists "nversegame").
developerEntities: Second Dinner Studios (developer, USA)
Nuverse / Nverse Game (publisher, ByteDance subsidiary, 2022-2025)
Skystone Games (publisher, USA, 2025-present)
notes: CONFIDENCE: high. Key framing: the GAME IS ALIVE — still operating globally, now published by Skystone Games with Second Dinner handling most publishing in-house. What "died" for a ByteDance graveyard is ByteDance's involvement (the Nuverse publishing relationship). Status set to "rebranded" to reflect the publisher change off ByteDance; "alive" or "acquired-shut" are defensible alternatives. "banned" would mislead, since the US ban was brief (~Jan 19-29, 2025) and fully reversed. ByteDance was only the PUBLISHER via Nuverse — it never owned the Marvel IP (Marvel/Disney) or the developer (Second Dinner). DATE NOTES: dateLaunched 2022-10-18 firmly sourced (global mobile launch + simultaneous PC early access; PC full release Aug 22, 2023). dateKilled 2025-01-28 = the Skystone announcement / formal end of the ByteDance/Nuverse relationship (Wikipedia explicit; some press dated Jan 29). Alternative kill date 2023-11-27 (ByteDance announced winding down Nuverse), but the relationship continued until Jan 2025. App Store ID 1592081003 stable across the publisher transition.
successor: Marvel Snap (published by Skystone Games / Second Dinner)

appStoreId: 1160056295 (global: https://apps.apple.com/app/mobile-legends-bang-bang/id1160056295). Separate US app under Skystone: "Mobile Legends: Bang Bang.US" id 6741568655 (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/mobile-legends-bang-bang-us/id6741568655)
description: A free-to-play 5v5 mobile MOBA, huge in Southeast Asia and Latin America. Not shut down: ByteDance agreed in 2026 to sell maker Moonton to Saudi Arabia for over $6B.
killedReason: Not shut down — divested. The game itself is still fully operational. The "death" is of ByteDance's ownership: after exiting high-budget gaming (Nuverse restructuring in late 2023, redirecting resources to generative AI), ByteDance agreed on March 20, 2026 to sell developer Moonton to Saudi Arabia's Savvy Games Group (owned by the Public Investment Fund) for more than $6 billion (~$2B gain over its ~$4B 2021 acquisition), pending regulatory approval. Separately, in January 2025 the game was briefly pulled from US app stores (Jan 19–21, 2025) under the Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act (PAFACA) due to its ByteDance ownership; it was restored after a Trump executive order, and US publishing was moved to Skystone Games to insulate it from the ByteDance-linked ban.
notes: Status nuance: NOT a shutdown/dead product. The game is fully alive — it was DIVESTED by ByteDance, not killed. Used schema code "acquired-shut" as the closest available value; for a graveyard UI this belongs in an "acquired/divested" bucket and ideally tagged "divested." dateKilled set to 2026-03-20 (date the Savvy sale was agreed/announced) to represent the end of ByteDance's ownership era; note ByteDance ownership formally ends only on deal completion, pending regulatory approval as of latest reporting (though Wikipedia already lists Moonton as a Savvy subsidiary). Launch: Android July 14, 2016 as "Mobile Legends: 5v5 MOBA" (high); pulled after a Riot Games copyright complaint; relaunched as "Bang Bang" with iOS release Nov 9, 2016 (high). ByteDance acquisition via Nuverse, March 2021, reported ~$4B, beating a Tencent bid (high; price not officially disclosed). PAFACA removal Jan 19, 2025, restored Jan 21 after a Trump EO delaying enforcement (high); US publishing shifted to Skystone Games with a separate US App Store listing id 6741568655. Savvy sale agreed/announced March 20, 2026, >$6B (high — Bloomberg/Reuters plus Yicai, GameDeveloper, esports.gg, escharts, PocketGamer.biz). Bloomberg not directly accessible (paywall) but corroborated by multiple outlets. Reached ~1.5B downloads and ~110M MAU with a major esports scene (M-Series World Championship, MSC).

name: 悟空问答 (Wukong Q&A)
description: ByteDance's Quora/Zhihu-style Q&A app under Toutiao. Despite ~RMB 1bn in subsidies it never dented Zhihu; ByteDance closed it in 2021 and folded it into Toutiao.
killedReason: Persistent failure to compete with Zhihu despite heavy subsidies (~RMB 1bn pledged for 2018 plus ~RMB 500m for top creators; cumulative spend cited up to ~RMB 2bn) and Toutiao's built-in traffic. Monthly active users averaged only ~800,000 in mid-2018 and collapsed to ~170,000 by July 2018 while Zhihu held tens of millions; community/operations-heavy products ran against ByteDance's traffic-and-algorithm-centric model. ByteDance de-prioritized the product in mid-2018, merging its team into Weitoutiao, and strategic focus shifted to short video (Douyin, Huoshan). The standalone app was retired in early 2021 as part of a broader cull of non-core products, and its Q&A function was folded back into the Jinri Toutiao app (revived as the 头条问答 feature), so the product was merged/absorbed rather than fully deleted — content, creator accounts, and the website continued under Toutiao.
publisher: Beijing ByteDance Technology Co., Ltd. (北京字节跳动科技有限公司) / Jinri Toutiao (今日头条) — published under ByteDance/Toutiao developer entities
developerEntities: Beijing ByteDance Technology Co., Ltd. (北京字节跳动科技有限公司)
Jinri Toutiao / Today's Headlines (今日头条)
Beijing ByteDance Network Technology Co., Ltd.
notes: All key dates independently re-verified. Standalone app/website launch (rebrand from 头条问答 to 悟空问答) confirmed June 26, 2017 by Guancha. App store removal Jan 20, 2021 and service cessation Feb 3, 2021 confirmed by Jiemian and kr-asia; shutdown announcement was ~Jan 14-15, 2021 (Caixin/kr-asia). The original feature 头条问答 launched ~April 2016 inside Toutiao. MAU averaged ~800,000 (80万) April-July 2018, then dropped to ~170,000 by July 2018 (kr-asia, Sina Tech). Subsidy: ~RMB 1 billion (10亿) pledged for 2018 plus ~RMB 500 million for top creators (kr-asia); cumulative spend of ~RMB 2 billion (20亿) cited by some Chinese press. Status classified as MERGED (folded into Jinri Toutiao / revived as in-app 头条问答 feature; website and creator content persisted) rather than fully dead. 2023 'revival' caveat: reports (Feb 2023, 36Kr/Jiemian/TMTPost) said ByteDance would relaunch a redesigned Wukong Q&A app, BUT Toutiao officially called the relaunch news '不实' (inaccurate) per IT之家 — treat as DENIED/unconfirmed, not a confirmed return. A separate related Q&A product 秒真问答 (Miǎozhēn Wèndá) was reportedly launched ~2023 embedded in Toutiao/Douyin. Could not retrieve an App Store numeric ID — China-market app, listing delisted; appStoreId left empty.

name: 好好学习 (Haohao Xuexi)
description: ByteDance's paid-knowledge app for adult self-improvement, a Dedao rival. ByteDance closed it in 2021 after weak sales and no breakout courses.
killedReason: Closed as part of a ByteDance business/organizational adjustment. The product had weak monetization and low commercial-conversion rates (repeat-purchase rates reported around 10% versus earlier projections of ~30%), poor ROI relative to core products (Douyin/Toutiao), and lacked the star-instructor IP and breakout ("出圈") courses that drove rivals like Dedao; signs of trouble (project editor-in-chief departure, followed by 20+ staff leaving in H1 2019) appeared as early as 2019. The shutdown coincided with a pre-Spring-Festival 2021 efficiency/restructuring drive in which ByteDance also wound down Wukong Wenda (悟空问答) and the Smartisan/Jianguo phone team. ByteDance officially framed it as a "normal business adjustment" (正常业务调整). On closure, user balances, memberships and purchased courses could be migrated (with user authorization) to the user's linked Jinri Toutiao (今日头条) account.
publisher: Beijing Zhending Technology Co., Ltd. (北京臻鼎科技有限公司)
developerEntities: Beijing Zhending Technology Co., Ltd. (北京臻鼎科技有限公司) — wholly-owned ByteDance subsidiary; same entity behind Fanqie (Tomato) Free Novel / 番茄免费小说 and Tomato Audio / 番茄畅听
ByteDance (字节跳动 / Beijing ByteDance Technology Co., Ltd.) — ultimate parent
notes: Launch date is the main uncertainty (medium confidence): July 2018 is the most-cited specific figure (confirmed verbatim by The Paper and Jiemian, which also carry the "500 courses / 1,000 audiobooks / four-section" detail), but The Paper's narrative and Sina/21jingji also describe a "late 2017" entry into education — likely an earlier soft/internal launch with a fuller public version in July 2018. Retained 2018-07 as best-supported specific date; 2017 alternative flagged. Shutdown facts are solid: closure notice issued Jan 5, 2021 (reported Jan 6); operations ceased Jan 20, 2021; assets migratable to linked Jinri Toutiao accounts. Developer 北京臻鼎科技有限公司 confirmed 100% ByteDance-owned and is the same shell behind Fanqie Free Novel / Tomato Audio. App Store numeric ID not findable — app was delisted from Chinese stores by/around Jan 2021; appStoreId left empty. Closed the same month as Wukong Wenda as part of the early-2021 restructuring.

name: 多闪 (Duoshan)
category: social / video messaging (originally a Snapchat-style disappearing-video messenger; now positioned as "Douyin chat companion" / camera-based social)
publisher: Beijing Douyin Technology Co., Ltd. (北京抖音科技有限公司) — current App Store seller; originally operated under Beijing Weibu Shijie Technology Co., Ltd. (北京微播视界科技有限公司). ByteDance/Douyin.
developerEntities: Beijing Weibu Shijie Technology Co., Ltd. (北京微播视界科技有限公司)
Beijing Douyin Technology Co., Ltd. (北京抖音科技有限公司)
Beijing Douyin Information Service Co., Ltd. (北京抖音信息服务有限公司; renamed from Beijing ByteDance Technology in May 2022)
ByteDance Ltd. / Douyin Ltd. (字节跳动 / renamed Douyin Ltd. May 2022)
region: China (mainland; originally required a Douyin account and a mainland Chinese phone number)
killedReason: ByteDance's standalone anti-WeChat messenger failed and was folded into the Douyin ecosystem. Tencent blocked Duoshan's link inside WeChat on launch day (Jan 15, 2019), it vanished from Tencent's Android store YingYongBao (~Mar 12, 2019), and a Tianjin Binhai court (Mar 20, 2019) barred ByteDance from using WeChat/QQ handles and avatars in Douyin/Duoshan. With the WeChat-challenger concept dead, v10.0.0 (Oct 27, 2021) ended standalone Duoshan-account login and forced users to merge into their Douyin account; old-version (≤6.8.0) maintenance ended Nov 30, 2021 — the same window ByteDance shut its sibling social app Feiliao (飞聊, ~Dec 2021). The app was later repositioned as a camera-first, BeReal-style "limited-time" real-photo social app (2022, v22.2.0), then reframed as "多闪-抖音聊天版" / Duoshan - Douyin Chat Version. After ~31 rapid iterations in 2025 and heavy in-Douyin promotion, it topped China's App Store social free chart from ~Nov 13, 2025 through end of November. So the original 2019 anti-WeChat product is dead/merged, while the app name and App Store ID live on as an actively iterated Douyin chat companion.
description: ByteDance's Snapchat-style messenger, one of three 2019 apps meant to topple WeChat. Tencent blocked it on day one; ByteDance folded it into Douyin in 2021.
notes: Launch date 2019-01-15 is high-confidence (Bloomberg, TechCrunch, SCMP, TechNode, Pandaily all agree). KEY NUANCE: this is NOT a clean shutdown. The 2019 anti-WeChat standalone concept was effectively killed and absorbed into Douyin around late 2021, but the same app/App Store ID (1435602536) is ALIVE today (v39.0.0, June 2, 2026) and actively iterated as a "Douyin chat companion," topping China's App Store social free chart from ~Nov 13, 2025. For a "Killed by ByteDance" graveyard, the honest treatment is status='merged': the original Duoshan (2019 disappearing-video WeChat challenger) was de-facto killed/folded into Douyin, while the app name/ID was later revived in a totally different form. dateKilled set to 2021-10-27 (v10.0.0 account-merge-into-Douyin; old-version maintenance ended 2021-11-30) as the death of the original concept — medium confidence on those exact 2021 dates (sourced from Chinese tech press: Tencent News, 36Kr, The Paper, woshipm recounting version history). Tencent-blocking dates (WeChat link blocked Jan 15 2019; off YingYongBao ~Mar 12 2019; Tianjin court injunction Mar 20 2019) are high-confidence (TechNode). The original Android package name 'my.maya.android' confirms the internal 'Maya' codename, consistent with ByteDance's Faceu/Maya social team. Publisher entity history (Beijing Weibu Shijie → Beijing Douyin Technology) is consistent with ByteDance's May 2022 corporate renaming wave.

name: 飞聊 (Feiliao / Flipchat)
publisher: ByteDance (北京字节跳动科技有限公司). A Chinese report claims the iOS build was submitted under a non-enterprise/personal developer account rather than ByteDance's corporate account (medium confidence; never confirmed by Apple or ByteDance).
developerEntities: ByteDance / 北京字节跳动 (Beijing ByteDance)
description: ByteDance's messenger-plus-groups app, its second swing at WeChat. Apple pulled it within two months; it never caught on, and ByteDance killed it in 2021.
killedReason: Persistent failure to gain traction in China's WeChat-dominated social market; team dissolved and reassigned internally in Dec 2021 as ByteDance retreated from non-core social businesses.
notes:
DATE-KILLED nuance: there are two distinct dates. (1) Effective death / team dissolution: December 2021 (set as dateKilled, 2021-12), when the remaining team was dissolved and reassigned internally — reported Dec 13, 2021 by SCMP citing 36Kr; the app was already pulled from most app stores by ~May 2021 and was no longer usable by late 2021. (2) Official website feiliao.com going offline: Chinese primary sources (Sina Finance, 极目新闻/ctdsb, 3elife, IT之家) date this to ~October 15-17, 2022, NOT end of 2021. So: team substantially dissolved end of 2021; website taken offline ~Oct 2022. Both high confidence.

App Store ID: NOT found. The original Feiliao iOS listing is long delisted; current apps.apple.com search hits resolve to unrelated apps. Left empty. The iOS build was removed from the App Store ~July 12, 2019 (fewer than 2 months after launch; TechNode reported it on that date, with a Feiliao spokeswoman saying they were "communicating with Apple"); Apple's reason was never publicly stated. A Chinese report claims the app was submitted via a non-enterprise/personal developer account — plausible contributing factor but medium confidence.

Android: remained available on Chinese third-party Android stores after the July 2019 iOS removal; downloads reportedly stopped around April 2020 (medium confidence on exact month). App pulled from most stores ~May 2021.

Publisher entity: ByteDance is certain (confirmed by TechNode, TechCrunch, Caixin, SCMP, KrASIA, Sixth Tone). Exact App Store developer-account name on the original listing is not confirmable (medium confidence on account specifics).

Mid-2021 Clubhouse-style audio relaunch is reported by SCMP and others but was brief; exact relaunch day not pinned (~July 2021).

name: 心图 (Xintu)
category: photo / image-based social community (Instagram-like / 类Ins社区)

killedReason: Failed to gain traction in a crowded Instagram/Xiaohongshu-clone space and received minimal internal support and resources from ByteDance (which was spreading effort across many simultaneous social products — Duoshan, Feiliao, etc.). Per Jiemian it had "天壤之别" (a world of difference) in resourcing versus Duoshan, and per SCMP it "failed to take off on the mainland and was discontinued." It quietly went offline ("上线半年就悄然离线") and was delisted from app stores about six months after launch, with no official announcement.
publisher: ByteDance (字节跳动) — specific App Store/Play developer entity not identifiable from available sources (likely a ByteDance subsidiary such as Beijing Microlive Vision / 北京微播视界, but unconfirmed for this app)
developerEntities: ByteDance / 字节跳动
notes: CONFIDENCE: medium overall, but ByteDance ownership and dead status are HIGH confidence (corroborated by Jiemian ×2, ThePaper, SCMP, PingWest, zh.Wikipedia). (1) LAUNCH DATE: January 2019, confidently. New corroborating detail: "心图问世只比多闪晚了十几天" (Xintu launched only ~10+ days after Duoshan); Duoshan launched Jan 15, 2019, so Xintu = late January 2019. Exact day not pinned; 2019-01 is the safe value. (2) SHUTDOWN DATE: No official announcement, no exact date. Jiemian: "上线半年就悄然离线" (~6 months after launch) => roughly mid-2019. Jiemian (6337089) also states "前者已经在应用商店下架" (delisted). dateKilled = 2019 (best estimate ~mid-to-late 2019). (3) REGION: SCMP says it failed "on the mainland." zh.Wikipedia lists 心图 (类Ins社区) under the MAINLAND CHINA discontinued (已停止运营) section — no discrepancy; mainland China is correct and consistent across all sources. (4) PUBLISHER / APP STORE ID: Not locatable; app delisted years ago, no archived listing surfaced. (5) SOURCE NOTE: The SCMP article (3184899) is primarily about a different 2022 app ("Kesong") and mentions Xintu only in one passing line. Valid corroboration but NOT a dedicated Xintu article — the two Jiemian articles are the strongest direct sources. (6) DISAMBIGUATION (critical): 心图 (Xintu, this dead 2019 photo-social app) must NOT be confused with 醒图 (Xingtu), ByteDance's still-active all-in-one photo-EDITING app (Android package com.xt.retouch) — different product, similar pinyin/characters. Also distinct from 新草 (Xincao, ByteDance's 2018 Instagram/Xiaohongshu-clone that was also discontinued ~1 year after launch).

name: 新草 (Xincao)
category: social commerce / lifestyle "种草" (zhongcao / product-seeding) content community
description: Toutiao's Xiaohongshu-style lifestyle app where users found products but left to buy. ByteDance closed it after a year, having never dented Xiaohongshu.
killedReason: Failed to gain traction against Xiaohongshu. ByteDance ran Xincao with the same strong-algorithm/strong-recommendation product logic as its Toutiao news app and buried the content-publishing entry point deep in the UI, which suppressed original UGC vitality and prevented a genuine community/grass-planting atmosphere from forming. Downloads dwindled to near zero from late January 2019, the app stopped updating after ~March 2019 (at times not even downloadable, citing "developer reasons"), and it formally ceased operations on 1 August 2019, roughly a year after launch. ByteDance kept pursuing the "种草" category afterward (Kesong/可颂 2022, Youshi/有柿 2023).

name: 值点 (Zhidian)
publisher: 北京空间变换科技有限公司 (Beijing Kongjian Bianhuan Keji Youxian Gongsi / "Beijing Space Transformation Technology Co., Ltd."), a wholly-owned (100%) subsidiary of 今日头条有限公司 (Jinri Toutiao Ltd. / ByteDance)
developerEntities: 北京空间变换科技有限公司 (Beijing Kongjian Bianhuan Keji Youxian Gongsi / Beijing Space Transformation Technology Co., Ltd.)
今日头条有限公司 (Jinri Toutiao Ltd.)
字节跳动 (ByteDance / Beijing ByteDance Technology Co., Ltd.)
killedReason: Failed to gain traction in China's discount e-commerce market against Pinduoduo. Zhidian copied Pinduoduo's factory-direct, sub-RMB200 low-price playbook but lacked Pinduoduo's key advantages: it only accepted Alipay (plus cash-on-delivery) and pointedly excluded WeChat Pay due to the ByteDance-Tencent rivalry, leaving it without WeChat's social/sharing ecosystem and group-buying (拼团) demand-aggregation mechanic. ByteDance commentary judged the Toutiao-system commerce experiments as offering "no eye-catching innovation." With weak momentum, ByteDance abandoned standalone commerce apps and folded commerce into Douyin/Toutiao; the parallel "放心购" (Fangxin Gou) line was split, with one branch upgraded into the in-Toutiao "值点商城"/Zhidian Mall and the other becoming the ad tool "鲁班"/Luban. The standalone app was quietly delisted the year after launch (2019).
description: ByteDance's discount shopping app inside Toutiao, built to answer Pinduoduo. Lacking WeChat Pay and group-buying, it flopped; ByteDance pulled it within a year.
notes: VERIFIED FROM SCRATCH. Publisher entity 北京空间变换科技有限公司 confirmed 100% owned by 今日头条有限公司 (Toutiao/ByteDance), legal rep Li Fei (李飞), 100万 registered capital, Beijing (Tianyancha/Qichacha). DATES: English press (TechNode, Yicai, SCMP, Caixin) all cluster the discovery/launch at Oct 15-17, 2018; Jiemian ("悄悄上线" / quietly launched "last month") supports a September 2018 soft launch. dateLaunched set to 2018-10 to reflect the strongest, most consistent sourcing (media-confirmed launch); the September soft-launch is plausible but rests on one indirect reference. SHUTDOWN: no exact date ever published; multiple Chinese sources (Sina Finance "字节跳动电商史", ifanr) say it was delisted "the following year" (2019) and ifanr confirms it could no longer be found in any app store. dateKilled = 2019. NUANCE: the standalone app is the dead product; the "值点商城"/Zhidian Mall brand persisted inside Toutiao's commerce stack. Do NOT confuse with unrelated "ValueX" (id6472241469).

Jinri Toutiao / Toutiao (今日头条) — operating brand
Beijing Douyin Information Service Co., Ltd. (北京抖音信息服务有限公司) — current App Store developer entity on the legacy listing
appStoreId: 517166184 (https://apps.apple.com/app/id517166184 — app delisted; listing inactive, confirmed via Apptopia as "内涵段子-精选搞笑图片、视频")
description: ByteDance's very first app (2012, before Toutiao), a hugely popular Chinese joke and meme community. China's regulator shut it for good in 2018 over vulgar content.
killedReason: Permanently shut down by Chinese government order on April 10, 2018 (regulator order announced April 9–11; Zhang Yiming's public apology published the morning of April 11). The broadcast regulator — variously credited to SAPPRFT / the State Administration of Radio and Television / the newly-forming National Radio and Television Administration (国家广播电视总局, NRTA/SART), reflecting the March 2018 government reorganization that was underway — cited content that was "vulgar and improper," that "collided with core socialist values," and that triggered strong resentment among internet users. It came amid the same content crackdown that suspended Toutiao's main news app and several others. Zhang Yiming published an open apology admitting the product "had gone astray," over-relied on recommendation algorithms while neglecting socialist-values guidance, accepted "all the punishment," and pledged to expand content moderators from 6,000 to 10,000.
notes: Core facts independently re-verified from scratch: ByteDance's first app (March 2012, by Zhang Yiming, predating Toutiao); permanently shut by regulator order on 2018-04-10 for vulgar content; Zhang Yiming public apology (moderators 6,000→10,000); Pipixia (皮皮虾) successor July/Aug 2018 with account sync. Launch day not documented — recorded as 2012-03. Date nuance: regulator action/announcement spans April 9–11; April 10 (per Caixin) used as the shutdown date and is well-supported. User-count discrepancy persists across sources (~200M registered / ~11M DAU per Chinese sources vs ~17M cited by Caixin English) — flagged, not resolved. Regulator naming varies (SAPPRFT vs SART/NRTA) due to the concurrent March 2018 government reorganization; both refer to the same broadcast authority. App Store ID 517166184 confirmed via Apptopia as the "内涵段子-精选搞笑图片、视频" listing; direct App Store fetch fell back to the editorial "Today" tab, consistent with the app being delisted. Status "banned" chosen because it was a forced government permanent shutdown, not a voluntary discontinuation.

notes: STATUS = ALIVE (high confidence) — Pipixia is NOT a graveyard tombstone. Independently re-verified: live App Store listing (iOS id 1393912676) shows version 6.1.5, last update March 9 2026, 4.9 stars / ~1.34M ratings, Entertainment category, available; Tencent app market (sj.qq.com, com.sup.android.superb) shows Android version 6.3.0, last update May 25 2026, ~23.5M downloads, no discontinuation signs. An active short-drama Lite/极速版 spinoff (com.sup.android.explore, v4.3.6) and a Pro/专业版 edition also exist. No shutdown, merger, or rebrand announcement found. LAUNCH DATE: 2018-07 (all reputable press — Yicai, TechNode, The China Project, 36Kr, Sina — date the launch to July 2018; the publishing shell 福建皮皮跳动科技有限公司 was incorporated Nov 28 2018, after launch). The applebyme.cn claim of a 2017 listing is uncorroborated and disregarded. RECOMMENDATION: List Pipixia only as a context/honorable-mention tied to the genuinely-dead Neihan Duanzi (内涵段子), the real "killed by ByteDance" entry (peak ~200M users; permanently shut by SARFT/NRTA in April 2018 for "vulgar" content). Pipixia was built by the same community team, shares a near-identical logo, and let users migrate their Neihan Duanzi accounts, greeting returnees as "the Nth golden shrimp to return home"; it drew ~700,000 registered users in its first month and stopped syncing with Toutiao's account system on Aug 9, 2018 to distance itself from the banned predecessor.

notes: All material claims independently re-verified. (1) ByteDance ownership confirmed by SCMP ("Beijing Shi Qu Wu Xian Technology Co Ltd, ByteDance's wholly-owned subsidiary"), Sina, Pandaily, DaoInsights, and 电商派; developer entity 北京识区无限科技有限公司 also operates ByteDance's 识区/Shiqu reading product. (4) Public test July 13, 2022, v1.3.0, no invite code (Sina/163/IT之家). (5) Removed from Apple/Huawei/Xiaomi app stores ~July 25, 2022 over safety/process review issues (Sina, July 25 2022). Removal attributed to internal "safety process/compliance" reasons; no named government regulatory directive was publicly identified. App Store numeric ID is genuinely unrecoverable — CN listing was pulled July 2022 and never restored. Slogan: "派对岛，没有人是一座孤岛" ("Party Island — no man is an island"). Despite metaverse framing in the press, a Douyin representative said it had "nothing to do with the metaverse" and was "purely a social product still being tested on a small scale."

name: 识区 (Shiqu)
description: A ByteDance reading app that dropped its algorithm for human-curated interest circles. Invite-only and slow to grow, it closed eight months after launch.
killedReason: Discontinued amid ByteDance product/business adjustments. After internal beta testing (内测) that began around October 2021, the app launched on Chinese app stores in April 2022, but was quietly pulled from those stores on July 26, 2022 (no reason given). On December 7, 2022 ByteDance issued a notice that Shiqu would cease operations and related services on December 12, 2022 — roughly eight months after its April 2022 app-store launch (and ~14 months after internal testing began). ByteDance never publicly stated a specific reason; press attributed it to the failure of its "App factory" (App工厂) strategy to find product-market fit, with the manual-curation reading model struggling to scale, weak cold-start content quality (much early content was reposted from Toutiao), the invite-code barrier limiting creator supply, and super-apps (WeChat, Douyin) dominating user attention.
publisher: ByteDance (字节跳动) — operating entity Beijing Shiqu Infinity Technology Co., Ltd. (北京识区无限科技有限公司), a Douyin-invested company; built by the Jinri Toutiao (今日头条) team
developerEntities: ByteDance (字节跳动)
Beijing Shiqu Infinity Technology Co., Ltd. (北京识区无限科技有限公司)
Douyin Vision Co., Ltd. (抖音视界有限公司) — trademark holder
Toutiao / Jinri Toutiao (今日头条) team
notes: Independently re-verified from scratch; ByteDance ownership and dead status both high-confidence and cross-corroborated by Western tech press (TechNode, Pandaily) and Chinese sources (ifanr, Jiemian/界面, Sina, Beijing Daily/北京日报, CBNData, Zhihu). KEY TIMELINE CORRECTION vs draft: internal beta testing (内测) began ~October 2021 (per ifanr, Beijing Daily, multiple Chinese reports), NOT April 2022. April 2022 was the OFFICIAL APP-STORE LAUNCH (ifanr report dated April 6, 2022 still described it as in invite-only 内测 stage). The draft's note framing "April 2022 internal-test launch" is inaccurate — April 2022 = public/store launch; Oct 2021 = internal test start. Removed from app stores July 26, 2022 (confirmed). Shutdown notice published December 7, 2022 (one source says 近日/Dec 6); operations officially ceased December 12, 2022 (confirmed). OWNERSHIP DETAIL ADDED: operating company is 北京识区无限科技有限公司 (Beijing Shiqu Infinity Technology Co., Ltd.), a Douyin-invested entity; "识区" trademarks held by 抖音视界有限公司 (Douyin Vision Co., Ltd.) — both confirm genuine ByteDance ownership. App Store numeric ID not findable (China-only, delisted July 2022, no live listing). Not available outside mainland China. Description tightened to killedbygoogle.com style (under 240 chars); the fuller original description is preserved here for reference: "Shiqu (识区, 'knowledge zone/district') was a text-based reading and content-community app from ByteDance, built around content aggregated from its Jinri Toutiao (今日头条) news platform. Tagline: '定义你的阅读宇宙' (Define your reading universe). Unlike ByteDance's signature algorithm-driven feeds, Shiqu emphasized human curation: users joined or created interest 'circles' (圈子, max 3 per user) led by community moderators called '区长' (zone leaders / district chiefs) who manually filtered and recommended content. It was invite-only and also functioned as an RSS reader, plus comments, likes ('点亮') and sharing. Commentators compared it to a mix of Douban Groups, Jike (即刻), Clubhouse, Baidu Tieba and WeChat Official Accounts — 'a more human Toutiao.'"

description: A fashion shopping app from Douyin's e-commerce arm, a Dewu/Xiaohongshu rival. ByteDance wound it down after ~9 months once Douyin's own shopping made it redundant.
killedReason: Underperformance and redundancy. Douyin already had built-in shopping and a priority "Mall" (商城) channel, so a separate fashion app was unnecessary; Box couldn't retain creators or content and brought no incremental users (per Sensor Tower ~50,000 DAU / ~350,000 MAU, far below rivals Dewu and Xiaohongshu). Amid ByteDance's 2022 cost-cutting, around Sept 8, 2022 the company stopped pushing updates and offered the team internal transfers. ByteDance publicly denied a "suspension," but the app was effectively wound down and never revived, with commerce kept inside the main Douyin app.
notes: Launch date 2021-12-16 confirmed by multiple Chinese sources (IT Home, Xueqiu, 36Kr, Jiemian) and English coverage. ByteDance ownership confirmed: published/operated by Beijing Microlive Vision Technology Co., Ltd. (北京微播视界), the standard Douyin-family App Store entity. Status is best described as "merged/wound-down" rather than a clean hard shutdown — there is NO single official shutdown announcement or exact delisting date. ByteDance stopped updating the app and offered team internal transfers around 2022-09-08; it publicly DENIED a "suspension" while not actively maintaining it, so dateKilled is given as 2022-09 (effective wind-down): high confidence on the abandonment, medium on treating Sept 2022 as a precise "kill" date. E-commerce stayed inside the main Douyin app and Douyin Mall channel. User figures: the rigorously sourced number across 36Kr, Jiemian and English press is Sensor Tower's ~50,000 DAU / ~350,000 MAU (Q1-Q3 2022); medium confidence on exact user numbers. Team lead reported as Xu Luran (徐璐冉), ex-Duoshan (多闪) — medium confidence. appStoreId left empty — could not independently confirm a live App Store numeric ID. IMPORTANT: Do NOT conflate with "抖音商城 / Douyin Mall," a separate later independent commerce app (also published by Beijing Microlive Vision) — different product.

killedReason: China's July 2021 "double reduction" (双减) policy and related rules banned for-profit K-12 academic tutoring, restricted online classes during holidays/weekends, required tutoring firms to register as nonprofits, and — critically for GoGoKid — prohibited hiring foreign personnel based outside China (境外外籍人员) to conduct training, which gutted GoGoKid's North-American-teachers-abroad model (the same blow that hit VIPKid). On Aug 5, 2021, GoGoKid issued an official "Letter to Parents" announcing it would, effective that day, fully suspend all live-class business, cancel scheduled bookings, and refund remaining class fees (as of 2021-08-02 24:00); remaining points/Unee coins had to be redeemed by Aug 31, 2021. The apps were pulled from Chinese app stores and ByteDance confirmed it would shut the platform down completely. Dali Education announced N+2 severance, and hundreds of edtech staff were laid off.
notes: Confidence HIGH on core facts (ByteDance ownership, dead status, May 2018 launch, Aug 5 2021 shutdown). Registered company is 北京比特智学科技有限公司 (智学 = "smart learning"), per consistent Chinese-language sources (eeo.com.cn, jiemian, leiphone); App Store's official romanization is "Beijing Bitezhixue Technology Co., Limited." Dali Education launch generalized to October 2020 (SCMP/Yicai/KrAsia) — exact day not independently re-confirmed. Foreign-teacher-abroad ban (境外外籍人员) well-sourced as a decisive factor; EdSurge frames it as one of several causes. Launch exact day not pinned (Yicai dated May 18 2018 says beta started "this month"). Shutdown Aug 5 2021 firmly sourced via official Letter to Parents (Worker.cn) + Jiemian + Bloomberg/TechNode; refunds through Aug 2 2021, points expired Aug 31 2021; corporate wind-down trailed into late 2021. Disambiguation: separate App Store app "GogoKids" (id1434962847) is NOT this product. App Store id 1411412060 still resolves as a listing but the app/service is dead. name: 瓜瓜龙 (GuaGuaLong)
category: Education (early childhood / preschool "enlightenment" AI tutoring, ages ~2-8)
publisher: 北京未来智学教育科技有限公司 (Beijing Future Zhixue Education Technology Co., Ltd.) — wholly ByteDance-owned (sole shareholder 北京字节奇点科技有限公司 / ByteDance Singularity Technology, in turn wholly owned by 字节跳动有限公司 / ByteDance Ltd.); marketed under the 大力教育 (Dali Education) brand
developerEntities: 北京未来智学教育科技有限公司 (Beijing Future Zhixue Education Technology Co., Ltd.) — App Store/WeChat account subject
北京字节奇点科技有限公司 (Beijing ByteDance Singularity Technology Co., Ltd.) — sole shareholder of Future Zhixue
大力教育 (Dali Education) — ByteDance education brand umbrella, launched Oct 29, 2020
字节跳动 (ByteDance Ltd.) — ultimate parent; Zhang Yiming named ultimate beneficiary
appStoreId: 1498345627 (https://apps.apple.com/cn/app/id1498345627) — for 瓜瓜龙启蒙; delisted from the App Store as of the 2026-04-15 shutdown
killedReason: Killed in two stages by China's education crackdown plus ByteDance's strategic retreat from non-core businesses. Stage 1 (2021): After the State Council's July 24, 2021 "double reduction" (双减) policy — which banned online tutoring for preschool-age children and barred foreign teachers — GuaGuaLong's core paid curriculum/short-term classes for ages 2-8 became non-compliant. Dali Education began mass layoffs in early August 2021 (N+2 compensation), cutting GuaGuaLong's roughly 8,000-strong frontline tutor force; the last GuaGuaLong employees were let go around November 2021 with compensation. The brand "transformed" rather than fully closing, and the app survived for existing/paid users. Stage 2 (2024-2026): A shutdown notice issued in October 2024 was quickly retracted. ByteDance then re-announced closure around April 2025 (a second notice with identical text but a new date) as it divested non-core education to focus on AI and overseas growth. Per the official notice, the 瓜瓜龙启蒙 app is delisted from app stores and stops service at 00:00 on April 15, 2026; paid course content migrates to the DoudouFox (豆豆狐) app for viewing until April 15, 2029, after which personal data is deleted/anonymized.
description: ByteDance's cartoon-dragon tutoring app for kids 2-8. China's 2021 "double reduction" crackdown gutted it; ByteDance later shut it for good.
notes: CONFIDENCE HIGH. ByteDance ownership independently re-confirmed: GuaGuaLong's WeChat/App Store account subject is 北京未来智学教育科技有限公司, whose investor changed from natural person Ding Huayong to 北京字节奇点科技有限公司 (wholly owned by ByteDance Ltd.; Zhang Yiming ~98.81% / ultimate beneficiary) — DoNews & PingWest. Launch date 2020-03-07 well-attested (Leiphone, Baidu Baike) for GuaGuaLong English; Chinese (语文) added ~late July 2020 per 21jingji. Zhang Ziyi endorsement confirmed (reported April 2021). Status genuinely DEAD: the app fully stops service and is delisted at 00:00 on 2026-04-15 per the official notice quoted by 36Kr and Tencent (a first Oct-2024 notice was retracted; re-announced ~April 2025). DATE-KILLED: product effectively died twice — 2021-11 for the original tutoring business (regulatory crackdown, last staff let go ~Nov 2021) and 2026-04-15 for the app's true end-of-service. dateKilled set to 2026-04-15 (true EOS); if the graveyard prefers the regulatory death, 2021-11 is equally defensible with a note that the app limped on for paid users until 2026. One Chinese headline (sycaijing) cited April 14, 2026, but the official notice text confirms April 15, 2026 00:00, with DoudouFox (豆豆狐) viewing until 2029-04-15. App Store id 1498345627 cited by the draft; could not independently re-confirm via iTunes lookup (app is delisted). Peak metrics (200,000+ students, ~8,000 tutors) are from Chinese press/company statements, not independently audited.

name: 清北网校 (Qingbei Online School)
publisher: ByteDance — Dali Education (大力教育). Android app package: com.edu.android.daliketang (the "daliketang"/Dali Classroom origin name was retained through the Qingbei rebrand). Operating entity for the app: Beijing Wanyou Yingli Technology Co., Ltd. (北京万友映力科技有限公司), confirmed via ITJuzi and Android app-store listings. Affiliated company name also seen: 清北华数教育科技（北京）有限公司. (Note: sibling Dali products 学浪/瓜瓜龙 are listed under a different shell, 北京引才视教科技有限公司 / Beijing ByteDance Qidian Technology — ByteDance used multiple education shells.)
developerEntities: ByteDance (字节跳动) / Toutiao (今日头条)
Dali Education (大力教育) — independent brand launched 2020-10-29, CEO Chen Lin (陈林)
Beijing Wanyou Yingli Technology Co., Ltd. (北京万友映力科技有限公司)
appStoreId: iOS App Store listing delisted/removed post-shutdown; numeric iOS id not findable as of 2026. Android package id: com.edu.android.daliketang (AppChina and Xiaomi store listings now return 404/redirect to homepage, consistent with discontinuation). Do NOT confuse with App Store ids 1661394870 / 1492213039 / 1598245651 (these belong to an unrelated company, Qingbei Daoyuan / 清北道远).
killedReason: Killed by China's July 2021 "double-reduction" (双减) policy banning for-profit core-subject (K-9 compulsory-education) after-school tutoring. ByteDance's Dali Education wound Qingbei down in stages: live large-class investment was cut from mid-2021 (June 7, 2021 internal all-hands signaled a pivot to AI recorded courses; reported by LatePost/晚点 ~June 25, 2021); middle-school courses were taken offline and enrollment paused starting Aug 2021, with layoffs (n+2 compensation) reported Aug 5-6, 2021 across Dali products (Qingbei, Guagualong, GoGoKid, Nipaiyi). Core compulsory-education subject tutoring had to cease by the regulatory deadline of Dec 31, 2021. The platform was briefly repositioned toward AI courses / a "smart learning platform," but in ByteDance's June 2022 education restructuring (~3,000 layoffs; Dali cut ~80%; announced ~June 18, 2022), the consumer K-12 online-tutoring products were fully wound down — closure/shutdown notices (停运公告) were posted on official sites for GOGOKID, Nipaiyi (你拍一), Qingbei Small Class (清北小班), and Tangyuan English (汤圆英语).
description: ByteDance's K-12 live-tutoring app, built from its 2019 buy of Hua Luogeng Online School. China's 2021 "double reduction" ban gutted Dali Education and closed it.
notes:
CONFIRMED: Genuinely a ByteDance product (Toutiao/ByteDance acquired 华罗庚网校 in May 2019, rebranded Qingbei; flagship K-12 product under Dali Education 大力教育). Killed by the double-reduction (双减) policy. isByteDance=true, high confidence.

ALIAS CORRECTION: The draft listed "清北小班 (Qingbei Small Class)" as a plain alias. Per 芥末堆 (June 19, 2022) and Sina Tech (June 23, 2022), the FOUR products that posted official shutdown notices (停运公告) were GOGOKID, 你拍一 (Nipaiyi), 清北小班 (Qingbei Small Class), and 汤圆英语 (Tangyuan English). 清北小班 is the more precise name of the terminal/sub-product that "ceased operations in 2021"; 清北网校 is the broader brand that transformed toward AI before the whole consumer K-12 line was gutted in June 2022. Closely related but not strictly identical — kept as a qualified alias.

ENTITY: Operating entity 北京万友映力科技有限公司 (Beijing Wanyou Yingli Technology Co., Ltd.) is now CORROBORATED via ITJuzi (itjuzi.com/company/34257974) and Android app-store listings — upgraded from the draft's "should be double-checked" caveat. Note sibling Dali products (学浪/瓜瓜龙) sit under a different shell (北京引才视教科技 / ByteDance Qidian), so don't generalize one entity to all Dali products.

DATE NUANCE (unchanged from draft, validated): two-stage death. Live large-class investment cut from mid-2021; core K-9 tutoring ceased by Dec 31, 2021 deadline; full closure in the June 2022 restructuring (~June 18, 2022). dateKilled=2022-06 is the most defensible for full closure; 2021-12 (or 2021) is also defensible if dating by cessation of the core tutoring function. Exact day-level date of Qingbei's own closure notice not pinned (medium-high confidence on 2022-06). dateLaunched=2019-05 reflects the ByteDance relaunch; the underlying startup dates to 2018-04 (华罗庚网校).

NAME: 'Qingbei' = 清北 = abbreviation of 清华+北大 (Tsinghua + Peking). Do NOT conflate with the unrelated "Qingbei Daoyuan" (清北道远) high-school apps (App Store ids 1661394870, 1492213039, 1598245651).

description: ByteDance's online-learning app, launched in 2020 and later rebuilt as a Douyin creator-course tool. The standalone app closed in 2024, absorbed into Douyin Classroom.
killedReason: The standalone Xuelang app was discontinued at 24:00 on July 31, 2024 and its course business merged into Douyin's in-app "抖音课堂" (Douyin Classroom), consolidating ByteDance's knowledge-payment services into Douyin proper. This came amid a broad 2024 contraction of China's paid-knowledge sector (Tencent Classroom and Taobao University also exited), driven by falling repurchase rates, regulatory pressure, and weak willingness to pay. An earlier blow came from the 2021 "Double Reduction" (双减) policy, which gutted ByteDance's Dali Education unit and forced Xuelang off K-12 livestreaming into vocational/creator courses.
notes: CONFIRMED ByteDance product (high confidence): App Store publisher 北京因材施教科技有限公司 is a subsidiary of 北京字节奇点科技有限公司 (ByteDance's education business-line entity); Android package com.bytedance.ep.android. SHUTDOWN: standalone app stopped service 2024-07-31 (24:00), absorbed into 抖音课堂 (Douyin Classroom), confirmed by Tencent News/QQ, Duozhi/多知, Sohu, 163, Sina, all quoting the official in-app notice. STATUS = "merged" (function absorbed into Douyin's in-app Classroom; standalone 学浪 brand/app disappeared). LAUNCH: ~mid-Aug 2020 (Jiemian/CLS/IT之家 reports dated Aug 13–14, 2020 announcing 学浪 + 清北小班); Dali Education brand publicly announced Oct 29, 2020. dateLaunched=2020-08 (medium confidence on exact day). The Nov 2021 event was a large Dali Education layoff round (~2,000 staff; Xuelang ~200 team cut by >half) forcing a pivot off K-12, NOT a full disbandment. App Store id 1515674937, last version 7.6.0 (July 15, 2024). Precision note: Alibaba dissolved 淘宝大学 and folded it into 淘宝教育; Tencent Classroom stopped course access Aug 1, 2024 and fully closed Oct 1, 2024.

killedReason: Shut down as part of ByteDance/Dali Education's retreat from the K-12 / language-tutoring sector following China's "double reduction" (双减) crackdown on after-school tutoring announced in July 2021. The 双减 rules barred online tutoring for preschoolers, restricted hiring of foreign teachers, and banned for-profit K-12 tutoring, gutting the business model. Tangyuan English (one of four Dali businesses: GOGOKID, 你拍一/YouPaiYi, 清北小班/Qingbei small class, and 汤圆英语) posted a suspension notice (停运公告) on its official site on Feb 18, 2022, with services having actually ceased before the end of 2021; a compensation/refund window was provided for paying users.

One TechNode-cn source attributed the immediate registrant to 北京点点看看科技有限公司 (Diandiankankan) — but that entity actually developed ByteDance's OTHER English apps (开言英语/OpenLanguage and 读白背单词); the 比特智学 attribution for Tangyuan English is correct. DATES: dateLaunched 2019-07 = internal-testing start (reported July 8-9, 2019); MoE filing (教育部备案) Dec 24, 2019 — exact public-release day not pinned (medium confidence on precise public-launch day, high confidence operating in 2019). dateKilled 2021 = service actually ceased before end of 2021 (functional death); public suspension notice (停运公告) posted Feb 18, 2022 — so a 2022 date refers to the announcement, not cessation. STATUS verification: App Store id 1450098745 now returns HTTP 404; Xiaomi listing (com.android.et.english) delisted; the official domain tangyuanyingyu.com has been dropped and re-registered as an unrelated sports-streaming site (看球通) — all consistent with the app being fully dead.

name: 你拍一 (NPY)
description: An animated math app teaching logic to kids 3-12 in small live classes. ByteDance bought it in 2020 and shut it in 2021 when "double reduction" outlawed it.
killedReason: Killed by China's July 2021 "double reduction" (双减) policy crackdown on the private after-school tutoring industry, which banned online tutoring for preschool-aged children and severely restricted curriculum-based tutoring — directly outlawing NPY's early-childhood online math-thinking classes (regulators treated such products as disguised "幼小衔接" preschool-to-primary training). As part of Dali Education's business adjustment announced August 5, 2021, NPY and GOGOKID were shut down (关停) — distinct from sister brands 清北网校 (Qingbei) and 瓜瓜龙 (Guagualong), which were pivoted/transformed (转型) rather than immediately closed. NPY announced it would fully suspend its live-streaming course business effective August 5, 2021 ("自8月5日起(含8月5日)将全面暂停直播课业务"), removed its app from app stores, and began refunding families: course balances as of 23:59 on Aug 2, 2021; classes on Aug 3-4 not charged; refunds within 20 working days. The shutdown came amid ByteDance's broader edtech retreat and major layoffs (N+2 compensation) across Dali Education in 2021.
publisher: Beijing Tianfu Tong Education Technology Co., Ltd. (北京天赋通教育科技有限公司); after Aug 2020 owned by ByteDance under its education unit, later branded Dali Education (大力教育)
developerEntities: Beijing Tianfu Tong Education Technology Co., Ltd. (北京天赋通教育科技有限公司)
ByteDance education unit / Dali Education (大力教育)
notes: VERIFIED from scratch. NPY is a real ByteDance product: acquisition announced August 21, 2020 (Sohu, EqualOcean); shut down August 5, 2021. "NPY" is the romanized initialism of "Nǐ Pāi Yī" (你拍一) used by English tech press (Pandaily, KrASIA). Former brand confirmed as 酷数学CoolMath (Cool Math), renamed to 你拍一 on Oct 10, 2018 (Sina Edu). Target age 3-12 per most sources; the Oct 2018 Sina rename article says 2-12. Operating company 北京天赋通教育科技有限公司, CEO 唐振华 (Tang Zhenhua), all confirmed. App Store numeric ID not locatable (app delisted 2021). Pre-acquisition ~20-30M monthly users is press-reported (Pandaily) — treat the exact figure as medium confidence. Description condensed to killedbygoogle.com style (<=240 chars).

name: 大力课堂 (Dali Ketang)
description: ByteDance's K-12 live-tutoring app, built on the acquired Qingbei Online School. The Dali Ketang name lasted two weeks before reverting to Qingbei Wangxiao.
killedReason: The "Dali Ketang" brand name was extremely short-lived. According to 36Kr and Leiphone, roughly half a month (~2 weeks) after its May 2019 launch ByteDance reverted the consumer brand name back to "Qingbei Wangxiao" (清北网校 / Qingbei Online School). The product itself continued under the Qingbei name, was later folded into ByteDance's standalone Dali Education (大力教育) brand unveiled at an Oct 29-30, 2020 launch event (alongside GoGoKid, Guagua Long/瓜瓜龙, Kaiyan/开言英语, etc.; CEO Chen Lin), and the underlying K-12 academic-tutoring operations were then wound down/restructured following China's July 24, 2021 "double reduction" (双减) crackdown that banned for-profit tutoring on the school curriculum for K-9. ByteDance began mass layoffs in its education unit around August 5, 2021; GoGoKid was shut down outright while Qingbei and Guagua Long pivoted toward non-academic education. The name died via rebrand in mid-2019; the underlying Qingbei product was wound down via the 2021 crackdown.
publisher: Beijing Wanyou Yingli Technology Co., Ltd. (北京万友映力科技有限公司) — a ByteDance / Toutiao entity (legal rep Li Fei)
developerEntities: Beijing Wanyou Yingli Technology Co., Ltd. (北京万友映力科技有限公司)
Beijing ByteDance / Toutiao (今日头条) education unit
Dali Education (大力教育) brand from Oct 2020
Qingbei Wangxiao (清北网校) operating entity
notes: VERIFIED ByteDance product (high confidence). Independently re-confirmed all core claims: (1) 36Kr exclusive (36kr.com/p/1723640053761, May 9 2019) confirms ByteDance/Toutiao launched 大力课堂, operated by 北京万友映力科技有限公司 (legal rep Li Fei), built on the acquired 清北网校 team, 100% Tsinghua/Peking U teachers, 小学数学思维+初中语文 summer courses, ~50,000 enrollment target, main-lecturer+TA model. TechNode (May 9 2019) corroborates it as ByteDance's third K-12 push after GoGoKid and aiKID. (2) The rapid rebrand back to 清北网校 is confirmed: Leiphone (Dec 2019) and 36Kr both report the brand reverted to 清北网校 after ~half a month ("在'大力课堂'推出半个月后...品牌名称改回'清北网校'"); exact day of the rebrand not pinned, only "~half a month after launch." (3) Acquisition price ~RMB 20 million corroborated by multiple Chinese sources. (4) Qingbei pre-history confirmed: founded April 2018 by Liu Yong (刘庸), angel round of RMB 10 million from ZhenFund (徐小平/真格基金). (5) Dali Education (大力教育) brand launch confirmed at an Oct 29-30, 2020 event (People's Daily/36Kr/Duozhi) — note the press conference was Oct 29, widely reported Oct 30; product matrix expressly included 清北网校. (6) 2021 双减 crackdown (policy issued July 24, 2021) and ~Aug 5, 2021 ByteDance education layoffs confirmed by Bloomberg/Caixin/Yicai/Global Times; GoGoKid shut down, Qingbei & Guagua Long pivoted to non-curriculum subjects. CAVEAT ON STATUS/DATE: status=rebranded with dateKilled=2019-05 reflects the death of the NAME (the documented, sourceable event). If the graveyard tracks the underlying product's operational death instead, that occurred ~July-Dec 2021 via 双减 (status could alternatively be 'banned'/regulatory or 'merged' into Dali Education, dateKilled 2021). App Store numeric ID for the ByteDance-era Qingbei/Dali Ketang app could not be confirmed (delisted after 2021 crackdown). Do not confuse with id 911639256 (学堂在线/XuetangX, Tsinghua MOOC, unrelated) or id 1466019487 (名师云课堂, unrelated). 'Dali Xiaoban' (大力小班, online small-class product launched Dec 2019) is a distinct sibling product. Minor spelling note: 映力 is more commonly romanized "Yingli" than "Yingyi" (draft had "Yingyi"); the Chinese 北京万友映力科技有限公司 is authoritative.
successor: Qingbei Wangxiao (清北网校 / Qingbei Online School)
